title: Ranunculus orbicans
intro: Ranunculus orbicans, also known as the golden buttercup, is a perennial plant species native to Europe and Asia, characterized by its bright yellow flowers and hairy, ovate leaves. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antiviral properties, offering potential health benefits in treating wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Ranunculus orbicans include saponins, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is commonly prepared as a decoction or tincture, often used to treat skin conditions, fever, and respiratory infections, while also being used as a topical treatment for wounds and skin irritations.
taxonomy: Ranunculus orbicans is classified within the family Ranunculaceae, commonly known as the buttercup family. This family comprises approximately 1,900 species of flowering plants, with Ranunculus orbicans being one of the many species within it. Ranunculus orbicans belongs to the genus Ranunculus, which contains around 600 species of plants. The Ranunculaceae family is a group of flowering plants that are generally found in temperate regions of the world. These plants are typically characterized by their showy, cup-shaped flowers and often grow in a variety of habitats, including wetlands and woodlands.

side_effects: Ranunculus orbicans is a plant species known for its potential toxicity, and its ingestion can cause severe side effects.
